revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23985667504|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Hiromine Shrine]][[ja:広峯神社]][[simple:Hiromine Shrine]][[zh:廣峯神社]]
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Himeji}}{{infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:Hiromine Shrine Pillars and stairway to main hall - 2008.jpg | deity = {{ill|Isotakeru|qid=Q10881556|1=Isotakeru-no-Mikoto}}; {{ill|Susanoo|qid=Q272993|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} | prefecture = {{ill|Himeji|qid=Q209801|1=Himeji}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34.873778|N|134.700333|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} {{nihongo|'''Hiromine Shrine'''|広峯神社}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Himeji|en|Himeji|qid=Q209801|lt=Himeji|1=Himeji}}.<ref name="nationwide-location-database-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Hiromine Shrine（Hyōgo） |url=https://www.jldb.bunka.go.jp/en/location/ |access-date=2023-10-27 |website=Nationwide location database |language=en}}</ref> It is one of the three main shrines of the [[Gion Faith|Gion cult]] alongside [[Yasaka Shrine]], and [[Tsushima Shrine]].<ref name="faure-2021a">{{Cite book |last=FAURE |first=BERNARD |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.2307/j.ctv1k3np30 |title=Rage and Ravage |date=2021-12-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |doi=10.2307/j.ctv1k3np30 |isbn=978-0-8248-8936-4}}</ref>{{rp|123}} Until the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}} the shrine was perceived as highly prestigious, on par with [[Kumano Taisha]], but little remains of its past glory.<ref name="faure-2021a" />{{rp||pages=124–125}} In contrast to other shrines of the [[Gion Faith|Gion cult]] the shrine is associated with agriculture.
It has branch shrines such as one in {{ill|Obama, Fukui|en|Obama, Fukui|qid=Q41773|lt=Obama, Fukui|1=Obama, Fukui}}.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Hiromine Shrine and the Gion Festival in Obama {{!}} Japan Heritage: Wakasa Province: A Cultural Heritage Linking the Sea to the Ancient Capital |url=https://www1.city.obama.fukui.jp/japan_heritage/bunkazai/detail.php?lang=EN&id=32 |access-date=2023-10-27 |website=www1.city.obama.fukui.jp}}</ref><ref name="電子図書館担当-2022a">{{Cite web |last=電子図書館担当 |date=2022-01-14 |title=The Ancient Documents of Hiromine Shrine {{!}} Kobe University Library - デジタルアーカイブ |url=https://lib.kobe-u.ac.jp/da/en/hiromine/ |access-date=2023-11-04 |language=en-US}}</ref> and many others.<ref name="電子図書館担当-2022a" />
== Historical significance == The shrine is between Enokuma Shrine in {{ill|Bingo Province|en|Bingo Province|qid=Q1049113|lt=Bingo Province|1=Bingo Province}} and Gion Shrine in Kyoto. This location helped spread the Gozu Tennō cult eastward.<ref name="faure-2021a" />{{rp||pages=124–125}}
According to the Nijūnisha chūshiki, which was compiled between 1532 and 1573 by [[Yoshida Kanetomo]], Gozu Tennō first made his presence known at {{ill|Akashi, Hyōgo|en|Akashi, Hyōgo|qid=Q26406|lt=Akashi|1=Akashi, Hyōgo}} in {{ill|Harima Province|en|Harima Province|qid=Q1047523|lt=Harima Province|1=Harima Province}}. From there, he shifted to Hiromine, subsequently relocating to Tōkōji in Kitashirakawa, Kyoto. By the Gangyō era (877–885), he had moved to the Kanjin-in of Kankeiji, now known as Gion Shrine.<ref name="faure-2021a" />{{rp||pages=124–125}}
== History ==
The ''{{ill|Harima Kagami|ja|播磨鑑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harima Kagami|1=Harima Kagami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11498749}}'' states, "In the reign of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|qid=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin|1=Emperor Sujin}}, a [[himorogi]] was built on Mount Hiromine." In {{ill|Tenpyō|en|Tenpyō|qid=Q1199879|lt=Tenpyō|1=Tenpyō}} 5 ({{ill|733|en|733|qid=Q23570|lt=733|1=733}}), {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉傑真傑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibi no Makibi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, upon returning from Tang China, felt divine power at this site while on his way back to the capital. He reported this to {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|qid=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu|1=Emperor Shōmu}}, leading to the shrine's establishment on Shirobe Hill in Tenpyō 6 (734), according to tradition. According to the records of {{ill|Susanoo Shrine (Fukuyama)|ja|素斢嚀神社 (福山市新市町戸手)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo Shrine (Fukuyama)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Kibi no Makibi enshrined the deity from Bingo Province. In {{ill|Tenroku|en|Tenroku|qid=Q1327229|lt=Tenroku|1=Tenroku}} 3 ({{ill|972|en|972|qid=Q23575|lt=972|1=972}}), the shrine was relocated to its current location at the summit of Mount Hiromine. While not listed in the ''{{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}'', the ''{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|qid=Q1052981|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}'' entry for {{ill|Jōgan|ja|賞角|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} 8 (866) mentions the "granting of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade, to the unranked Susanoo Shrine of Harima Province," believed to refer to this shrine.
The deity {{ill|Gozu Tennō|en|Gozu Tennō|qid=Q11570247|lt=Gozu Tennō|1=Gozu Tennō}} was highly significant in {{ill|Onmyōdō|en|Onmyōdō|qid=Q1340540|lt=Onmyōdō|1=Onmyōdō}}, and Kibi no Makibi, said to have been an {{ill|Onmyōji|en|Onmyōji|qid=Q2274163|lt=onmyōji|1=Onmyōji}}, is thought to have established Hiromine Shrine with close ties to Onmyōdō. Worship of Gozu Tennō, influenced by {{ill|Goryō|en|Goryō|qid=Q3273210|lt=goryō belief|1=Goryō}}, involved appeasing a potentially harmful deity to ward off {{ill|epidemics|ja|疫病|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/epidemics|1=ekibyou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17239211}} and {{ill|calamities|ja|災厄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/calamities|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 厄年|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 厄年|1=Yakudoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1587005}}. The {{ill|Gion cult|en|Gion cult|qid=Q11588425|lt=Gion faith|1=Gion Faith}} of {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|en|Yasaka Shrine|qid=Q692714|lt=Yasaka Shrine|1=Yasaka Shrine}} is particularly well-known, but at this shrine, the focus was on prayers for abundant {{ill|rice cultivation|ja|稲作|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rice cultivation|1=Rice Cultivation|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q734080}}. This practice, known as "Hiromine faith," underscores the shrine's historical role as a deity of {{ill|Agriculture|en|Agriculture|qid=Q11451|lt=agriculture|1=Agriculture}}.
In {{ill|Jōgan|ja|賞角|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} 11 ({{ill|869|en|869|qid=Q23917|lt=869|1=869}}), it is said that Gozu Tennō (Susanoo) was transferred from this shrine to {{ill|Gion|en|Gion|qid=Q926312|lt=Gion|1=Gion}}, the current Yasaka Shrine in Kyoto. A document from {{ill|Jōō|ja|賞憲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} 2 ({{ill|1223|en|1223|qid=Q5399|lt=1223|1=1223}}) refers to "the Gion head shrine, Hiromine Shrine of Harima Province." This has led to the shrine being considered the original and main shrine of Gion worship, though disputes with Yasaka Shrine over this claim persist. Sites linked to the deity's transfer from Hiromine to Yasaka Shrine include {{ill|Gion Shrine (Hyōgo)|lt=Gion Shrine (Hyōgo)|lt=Gion Shrine (Kobe)|ja|祇園神社 (神戸市兵庫区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine (Kobe)|qq=}}, {{ill|Nanba Yasaka Shrine|ja|難注八坂神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanba Yasaka Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka|1=Ōsaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11439906}}, and {{ill|Okazaki Shrine|ja|岡崎神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okazaki Shrine|qq=}} in Kyoto. Other branches, such as {{ill|Owase Shrine|ja|尾騎神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owase Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|qid=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}}, also trace their origins here.
Until the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era}} {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu bunri|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神仏分離|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神仏分離}}, the shrine was known as Tennōzan Zōfukuji or Hironezan Zōfukuji and was under the control of {{ill|Kanei-ji|ja|東參山康永寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanei-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|徳川小族家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} temple.
[[File:Ruin of Oshi Yashiki.JPG|thumb|right|200px|Remains of the shrine family’s (oshi yashiki) estate within the Hiromine Shrine precincts.]]
== Shrine Families and Decline ==
During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}} to {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|宣地時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, as was common with many shrines, the shrine priests held positions as {{ill|gokenin|ja|審家人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gokenin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Jitō|en|Jitō|qid=Q1329805|lt=jitō|1=Jitō}}, contributing to the shrine's prosperity. They even participated in the {{ill|Battle of Minatogawa|ja|湯岳水戦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Minatogawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, siding with the {{ill|Northern Court|en|Northern Court|qid=Q1201819|lt=Northern Court (Japan)|1=Northern Court}}.
Originally, the shrine families (''shake'') numbered 75. However, due to the turmoil of the {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|海国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eiroku era|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} wars, their influence waned. By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|治終|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the number stabilized at 34 families, known as the "Hiromine 34 shake," although by the {{ill|Classical Chinese|en|Classical Chinese|qid=Q37041|lt=Kanbun|1=Classical Chinese}} and {{ill|An'ei|ja|安喪|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An'ei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} eras, only 25 families remained. Key families included the Hirone, Koizuka, Uozumi, Sugiyama, and Taniguchi.
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治縛権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Restoration|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, significant reforms, such as the 1871 confiscation of shrine lands and the abolition of hereditary positions, drastically altered the structure of the shrine. Many shrine families transitioned to professions outside the religious sphere.
== Pilgrimage and Routes ==
Pilgrims from {{ill|Harima Province|en|Harima Province|qid=Q1047523|lt=Harima Province|1=Harima Province}} traditionally visited Hiromine Shrine before embarking on their {{ill|Ise pilgrimage|ja|伞供行駐、招拍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise pilgrimage|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. This "double visit" custom lasted until the early {{ill|Shōwa period|ja|明治決齊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa period|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Several routes lead to the mountaintop shrine, including a primary southern route from {{ill|Shirakuni|ja|淡幸村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirakuni|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. == Shrine Families and Decline ==
The shrine families (''shake'') peaked at 75 in number but declined to 34 by the {{ill|Eiroku|en|Eiroku|qid=Q1143341|lt=Eiroku|1=Eiroku}} era. By the {{ill|Kanbun (era)|en|Kanbun (era)|qid=Q1146228|lt=Kanbun|1=Kanbun (era)}} and {{ill|An'ei|ja|安永|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An'ei|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 安永 (元号)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 安永 (元号)|1=An'ei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1193910}} eras, the number further decreased, though the "34 shake" framework remained, with some families managing multiple shake lands. Key families included the Hirone, Koizuka, Uozumi, Sugiyama, and Taniguchi. Other families such as the Kaneda, Shibata, and Utsumi were descendants of prominent retainers or samurai who became shrine families.
In {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} 4 ({{ill|1871|en|1871|qid=Q7746|lt=1871|1=1871}}), the government confiscated the shrine's lands, and hereditary roles were abolished. Many shrine families transitioned to other professions or left the mountain. During the postwar era, shifts in agricultural practices and the economy further diminished the shrine's importance. Today, only two shrine family residences remain intact: the Koizuka and Uozumi residences, both closed to the public.
== Worship and Routes ==
Hiromine Shrine has been a central point for pilgrims in Harima, who traditionally visited before embarking on pilgrimages to Ise Shrine. This practice, called "double visits," continued until the early {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|qid=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} period.
Several trails lead to the mountaintop shrine. The primary southern route, known as the "front slope," originates in {{ill|Shirakuni|ja|白国 (姫路市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirakuni|1=Shirakuni (Himeji)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q132044248}}. Another, the "rear slope," starts from {{ill|Hirano|ja|北平野 (姫路市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirano|1=North Hirano|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q132044236}}. Additional trails from the east, west, and north connect with local villages or monasteries.
== Kibi no Makibi and the Hiromine tradition == [[Kibi no Makibi]] (695–775) founded the Hiromine Shrine.<ref name="電子図書館担当-2022a" /> He was an [[Onmyōji]] in the Chinese royal court and when he returned to Japan he spent a night in Hiromine. In the year 733 . That night, he dreamed that a nobleman appeared to him. He said Kotan refused him hospitality and had found shelter in Somin Shōrai’s house. But since then he had been wandering, and remembering a pact made with Kibi no Makibi in China, he had now come to see him. The role of Kibi no Makibi is significant in this context because he posthumously became the “ancestor” of Onmyōdō and also played an instrumental role in the development of the legend of [[Abe no Seimei]].<ref name="faure-2021a" />{{rp||pages=124–125}}
Later that year he built the beginnings of the shrine. The next year the main building was built.<ref name="nationwide-location-database-2023a" /><ref name="電子図書館担当-2022a" /> It was moved to the present location in 972.<ref name="電子図書館担当-2022a" />
== Onmyōji influence == In the ninth and tenth centuries, onmyōji, particularly those from Harima province, performed rituals focusing on deities of pestilence. Harima was renowned for onmyōji who operated outside the traditional {{interlanguage link|Yin-Yang Bureau|ja|陰陽寮}}. {{ill|Ashiya Dōman|en|Ashiya Dōman|qid=Q11641735|lt=Ashiya Dōman|1=Ashiya Dōman}} was prominent enough to rival [[Abe no Seimei]].<ref name="faure-2021a" />{{rp||pages=124–125}}
The {{ill|Genkō Shakusho|en|Genkō Shakusho|qid=Q4153225|lt=Genko shakushō|1=Genkō Shakusho}} attributes the Indian ascetic, [[:en:Hōdō Sennin|Hōdō Sennin]], with promoting the Hiromine Shrine's cult. As per this legend, [[Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tenno]] appeared to Hōdō Sennin as a protector against misfortunes during his time at Ichijōji on Mount Hokke. In another instance documented in the Saimyōji engi, when Hōdō Sennin was preparing to exorcize a malevolent tree stump, Gozu Tennō manifested as an old man riding a yellow ox and requested the stump. Following Hōdō Sennin's refusal, Gozu Tennō granted him land to establish a temple and further the (Senju) Kannon's cult in {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|qid=Q843882|lt=Ōmi province|1=Ōmi Province}}.<ref name="faure-2021a" />{{rp||pages=124–125}}
Gozu Tennō's is represented as an old man because he represents a [[landlord deity]] giving land to monks to build a temple.<ref name="faure-2021a" />{{rp||pages=124–125}}
He is also often presented as a god of war.<ref name="faure-2021a" />{{rp||pages=124–125}}
== References == {{reflist}}

[[ja:広峯神社]] [[ja:広島神社]] [[en:Hiromine Shrine]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Hiromine-jinja}} [[zh:廣峯神社]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Hiromine-jinja}} [[zh:廣峯神社]] <!--zhwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11484399}} [[Category:Agriculture in Japan]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:Gion cult]] [[Category:Gion shrines]] [[Category:Himeji, Hyōgo]] [[Category:Hyogo Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties in Himeji City]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Kokushi genzaisha]] [[Category:Kuroda Yoshitaka]] [[Category:Municipal Tangible Cultural Properties in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Himeji City]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
